summary: Aayush is a final-year accounting student living with parents in London who presents himself as a heavy delivery user but whose actual behavior is more home-cooking oriented. He's highly engaged with social media (8-9 hours daily) and positions himself as deal-savvy and convenience-driven, yet his diary reveals a more traditional eating pattern with frequent home meals and only occasional delivery orders. His communication style is enthusiastic but sometimes contradictory - he claims to cook 'once a week' while actually cooking most meals at home. He's strategically loyal to Uber Eats due to the 5% cashback from Uber One, requiring a £3-4 price difference to switch platforms. His relationship with food delivery appears more aspirational than actual - he wants to be seen as a modern, convenience-oriented student but his behavior suggests he's still quite dependent on family meal structures.
personality_notes: Talkative and eager to please, but somewhat scattered in responses. Shows self-awareness about potentially excessive behaviors (social media use). Tends to give aspirational answers that don't always match reality. Friendly and open, but occasionally needs questions repeated.
food_relationship: Presents as convenience-driven delivery user but actually relies heavily on family home cooking with strategic, deal-focused delivery ordering.

key_moments.description: Describes typical day routine in detail, revealing structured family meal times
key_moments.significance: Shows actual dependence on family food structure despite claims of independence

key_moments.description: Admits to 8-9 hours daily social media use with self-conscious preface
key_moments.significance: Reveals high digital engagement and self-awareness about potentially excessive behavior

key_moments.description: Explains loyalty to Uber due to 5% cashback despite price differences
key_moments.significance: Shows sophisticated understanding of subscription economics and loyalty drivers

what_not_said: Specific financial pressures despite living with parents
Details about family dynamics around food decisions
initial_hypotheses: Subscription benefits create stronger loyalty than claimed price sensitivity
Family living situation provides food security that enables 'experimental' delivery ordering
Social media influence on food choices may be overstated compared to convenience factors
questions_for_coding: How do subscription mechanics actually influence ordering frequency vs. stated preferences?
What role does family food security play in delivery ordering confidence?
How do students rationalize delivery spending when living with parents?
diary_summary: Actually cooked at home for 15/21 meals with only 2 delivery orders across 7 days, frequently skipped lunch due to schedule pressures, and showed strong routine around family breakfast and dinner times.
say_do_gaps: Claims to cook 'once a week' but diary shows regular home cooking
Estimates 2-3 delivery orders per week but only placed 2 in diary week
Positions himself as convenience-driven but shows strong adherence to family meal routines
Claims independence in food decisions but diary shows frequent family meals
